summary: Mission Control Queue tracks larger local mission batches with copyable prompts, state, gates, review checkpoints, batch history and returned run output before any automatic thread spawning or connector writes are approved. MCQ-010 remains the latest validated batch, but claims/product/legal work is now locked and the next direction is MCQ-011 machine-runtime capability.
gates: no automatic thread creation without Jonas approval
no paid API spend without approval
no product/legal/customer-facing claims without source approval

batches.title: Parallel workstreams without babysitting
batches.goal: Make Frankly OS better at running multiple larger workstreams without Jonas having to babysit every chat.
batches.context: Agent Brief Builder is implemented locally. Frankly OS has local routes, skills, site data feeds, surface gates, static QA and browser QA evidence. The next layer should queue bigger missions before any automated thread spawning.
batches.outcome: A supervised Mission Control Queue with copyable prompts, visible gates, review checkpoint and OS integration path.
batches.checkpoint: Review the four local mission outputs and decide whether the pattern is useful enough for a real multi-chat pilot.
batches.nextMove: MCQ-001 passed agent review for local use. Keep automation gated and use the pattern on MCQ-002.

review.decision: Useful enough for the next local manual Mission Control batch, but not Jonas-accepted automation.
findings: The queue is useful as a local operating board.
Automatic thread spawning remains approval-gated.
Run output should stay visible before integration.
The next proof should be a real operational batch.

run.summary: The first Mission Control batch was run locally as four mission outputs. It validates the queue structure, but not yet real parallel multi-chat execution.
run.decision: Keep automatic thread handoff gated. Use the queue manually on one real workstream before designing automation.
findings: Mission Control should show returned mission output, not only prepared prompts.
The honest state after this local run is review-ready, not integrated.
The next proof should be a real product, partner, growth or OS batch.
Automatic thread creation remains a separate Jonas-approved layer.

missions.purpose: Define the operating model for parallel Frankly OS workstreams.
missions.promptGoal: Design how larger mission batches should be split, queued, reviewed and integrated without creating constant user interrupts.
missions.expectedOutput: Mission model, queue fields, review cadence, approval gates and suggested next build order.
missions.validation: Check against existing Agent Brief Builder route, surface gates and follow-up ledger.
missions.stopRule: Stop before proposing automatic thread spawning as enabled behavior.

missions.purpose: Build the next local Mission Control surface or data artifact.
missions.promptGoal: Implement the queue as a local OS artifact with copyable mission prompts, state, gates and review checkpoints.
missions.expectedOutput: Changed local files, data artifact, site surface and QA notes.
missions.validation: Run local static QA and browser QA if site files, CSS, data or scripts change.
missions.stopRule: Do not publish, install plugins, call paid APIs or create threads automatically.

missions.purpose: Check whether the queue reduces micro-management risk.
missions.promptGoal: Review the Mission Control Queue for clarity, autonomy, gate safety, prompt quality and missing evidence.
missions.expectedOutput: Findings, risks, missing tests, confidence level and recommended fixes.
missions.validation: Compare rendered UI, data artifact, route and skill against the review contract.
missions.stopRule: Do not rewrite the implementation unless asked; report prioritized issues first.

missions.purpose: Fold the useful pattern back into Frankly OS.
missions.promptGoal: Update OS reports, README, live data, surface gates and follow-up ledger after the queue is validated.
missions.expectedOutput: Filed report, updated status artifacts, follow-up decisions and next recommended work.
missions.validation: Verify JSON parses, generated feeds are current and local QA still passes.
missions.stopRule: Do not mark automated handoff complete unless it has actually been built and approved.

batches.title: Partner activation kit pilot
batches.goal: Create a review-ready partner activation kit structure for a representative consumer electronics retail partner without external sharing or unsupported claims.
batches.context: Frankly has a test-next partner activation route, partner kit activation skill, product marketing context, copy review, coverage clarity and publishing gate. This batch should validate Mission Control on a real operational workstream, not more meta-system work.
batches.outcome: A practical internal partner kit with audience split, asset list, checkout copy drafts, seller lines, FAQ, support path, approval gates and first 1-90 day activation plan.
batches.checkpoint: Hold MCQ-002 as a review-ready internal draft until Jonas's new partner deck produces fresh learning.
batches.nextMove: Park partner-kit templating until the new partner deck learning lands. Let MCQ-003 carry the machine-core priority shift next.

parking.reason: Jonas is building a new partner deck in parallel, and that deck may change the partner narrative, proof needs and activation structure.
parking.resumeWhen: New partner deck learning is available for synthesis.

run.summary: The second Mission Control batch was run locally on a real partner activation workstream. It produced a representative internal partner kit while keeping external use gated.
run.decision: Keep the kit as an internal review draft and park templating until Jonas's new partner deck produces fresh learning. Do not use it externally until a real partner, product facts, commercial terms, support path and approvals are supplied.
findings: Partner activation is a good first non-meta Mission Control test.
The kit is useful as an internal structure, not approved partner-facing copy.
Coverage, commercial terms, support owners and logo rules remain source gaps.
Audience separation makes the kit reusable across leadership, sellers, support, customers and operators.
Partner-kit templating should wait for the new partner deck learning.
The next useful batch is machine core.

missions.purpose: Define the partner activation operating model, audience split, kit architecture and gates.
missions.promptGoal: Use the partner activation route to design a representative electronics retail partner kit that is useful internally but blocked from external use.
missions.expectedOutput: Product marketing context, partner job, audience split, kit architecture, source needs, mission split and approval gates.
missions.validation: Check against product-marketing context, partner kit activation, copy review, coverage clarity and publishing gate requirements.
missions.stopRule: Stop before naming a real partner, inventing commercial terms or approving customer-facing claims.

missions.purpose: Draft the representative partner activation kit.
missions.promptGoal: Create the internal kit with one-liner, product description placeholder, checkout copy drafts, seller lines, FAQ, support path, asset list, launch checklist and 1-90 day activation plan.
missions.expectedOutput: Review-ready partner kit draft with placeholders for product/legal, commercial, support and partner-specific facts.
missions.validation: Check that product claims are sourced or marked, audiences are separated, support path is visible and external use is gated.
missions.stopRule: Do not publish, email, sync, Slack, deploy or write partner-facing material externally.

missions.purpose: Risk-check the kit for unsupported claims, partner usability and approval gaps.
missions.promptGoal: Review the partner kit against the route quality door and identify what must be fixed before a real partner pilot.
missions.expectedOutput: Prioritized findings, quality-door table, risks, confidence and recommended fixes.
missions.validation: Verify audience separation, support path, marked placeholders, external sharing gate and no hidden automation.
missions.stopRule: Do not rewrite into external-ready copy without approved facts.

missions.purpose: Fold the partner activation batch back into Frankly OS.
missions.promptGoal: Update Mission Control, site feed, reports, README, live data and follow-up ledger so MCQ-002 is visible as a review-ready local run.
missions.expectedOutput: Filed report, updated queue state, browser/static QA notes, follow-ups and next recommended work.
missions.validation: Verify JSON parses, feeds are synced, local QA passes and browser QA proves the batch selector and run output render.
missions.stopRule: Do not mark partner material approved for external use.

batches.title: Machine core priority shift
batches.goal: Shift Frankly OS energy from building additional site pages to strengthening the operating machine: run state, Mission Control lifecycle, memory, evidence, workflow eval and gated handoff.
batches.context: Jonas wants more energy spent on the machine than on diverse pages. Frankly OS already has Agent Brief Builder, Mission Control Queue, a loop runner, agentic-loop route, follow-up ledger, site data feeds and static QA. MCQ-003 should improve the operating layer without creating a new page.
batches.outcome: A machine-core priority map, active batch state, updated follow-up priority and a local run record that makes the machine-first decision durable.
batches.checkpoint: Review whether the next work should evaluate and harden Mission Control plus loop runner before any new page or automation layer.
batches.nextMove: Use MCQ-004 to run workflow eval and install Prompt Agent v0.1 as a local mission-start layer.

run.summary: The third Mission Control batch shifted the next priority toward the Frankly OS machine rather than additional site pages.
run.decision: Machine core is the next priority. New pages are maintenance-only unless they expose or validate the machine. Partner-kit templating remains parked pending new partner deck learning.
findings: Jonas wants machine energy prioritized over new page builds.
The machine is the chain from intake to route, batch, loop, evidence, eval and memory.
New pages should be built only when they expose or validate machine state.
Partner-kit templating remains parked until new partner deck learning lands.
The next useful step is workflow eval of Mission Control plus loop runner.
Automation remains gated until the supervised machine proves itself.

missions.expectedOutput: Machine definition, priority shift, state language, gates and recommended next move.
missions.validation: Check against agentic-loop runtime route, Mission Control Queue, loop runner and workflow eval skill.
missions.stopRule: Do not propose new site pages as the default next build.

missions.expectedOutput: Machine-core JSON artifact and operating rule for future work.
missions.validation: Ensure the artifact is valid JSON and included in static QA.
missions.stopRule: Do not add a new page unless needed to expose machine state.

missions.expectedOutput: Prioritized findings, quality-door table, risks and recommended fixes.
missions.validation: Compare the artifact and queue changes against workflow eval and agentic-loop rules.
missions.stopRule: Do not approve automation or page expansion.

missions.purpose: Fold the machine-core priority shift back into Frankly OS.

missions.expectedOutput: Filed report, updated queue state, synced feed, QA evidence and next recommended work.
missions.validation: Verify JSON parses, feeds are synced, local QA passes and the loop run card is completed.
missions.stopRule: Do not mark supervised handoff or automation complete.

batches.title: Workflow eval + Prompt Agent v0.1
batches.goal: Evaluate whether Mission Control plus loop runner reduces babysitting, then add a local prompt-agent layer that turns broad intentions into larger mission prompts.
batches.context: Jonas wants to spend more energy on the machine than on building pages. Frankly OS has Agent Brief Builder, Mission Control Queue, MCQ-001 through MCQ-003, loop runner, workflow eval skill, follow-up ledger and static QA. The next layer should create stronger starting prompts without spawning chats automatically.
batches.outcome: Prompt Agent v0.1 exists as a local blueprint and generator script, backed by workflow eval and a generated sample prompt pack.
batches.checkpoint: Review whether the generated mission pack is good enough for one real workstream test before designing supervised thread handoff.
batches.nextMove: Use MCQ-005 to create a machine handoff packet and make resume behavior explicit before supervised thread handoff.

run.summary: The fourth Mission Control batch evaluated the current machine and added a deterministic local prompt-agent generator for larger mission prompts.
run.decision: Adjust and promote the local Mission Control plus loop-runner pattern. Use Prompt Agent v0.1 locally, but keep automatic thread spawning and connector writes gated.
findings: The machine is useful enough for local promotion, but not automatic orchestration.
Prompt generation should be deterministic and local before any thread spawning.
The generator reduces repeated prompt scaffolding and forces context, gates, artifacts and validation into each mission.
Resume state still needs a future handoff packet so active work can restart faster.
The next proof is one real large workstream generated by Prompt Agent v0.1.

batches.title: Machine Handoff + Resume Packet v0.1
batches.goal: Harden Frankly OS machine state and resume behavior so future chats can continue without rebuilding context.
batches.context: Prompt Agent v0.1 generated this real machine workstream. Frankly OS has MCQ-001 through MCQ-004, a loop runner, prompt-agent blueprint, follow-up ledger, machine-core priority map, local static QA and Mission Control. The missing layer is one compact read-first handoff packet.
batches.outcome: Machine Handoff v0.1 exists as structured state plus a read-first packet for future chats.
batches.checkpoint: Review whether future chats should start by reading the handoff packet before any new workstream.
batches.nextMove: MCQ-006 scored the first resumed handoff proof, and MCQ-007 completed the first non-meta product clarity business proof. Use the approval pack to collect product/legal source material before supervised thread handoff design.

run.summary: The fifth Mission Control batch used Prompt Agent v0.1 on a real machine workstream and created a current handoff packet for future chats.

findings: Prompt Agent v0.1 can generate a real machine workstream prompt pack.
Future chats now have one read-first handoff packet.
Handoff state records active batch, gates, parked work, read order and next actions.
The packet reduces context rebuilding risk but still needs a fresh workstream test.
Automatic thread creation remains gated until handoff proves useful.

Channel reuse should happen one approved channel at a time.
No customer-facing copy was changed.

batches.title: Machine State Consistency Guard v0.1
batches.goal: Add a local read-only guard that detects drift across Mission Control, handoff state, live data, site feed, loop state, prompt packs, evidence and gates.
batches.context: Started after MCQ-007 proved Machine Handoff v0.1 on a non-meta product clarity workstream. The next machine improvement is to catch stale active batch references, stale next moves, missing artifacts, feed drift and unclosed loop runs before future chats inherit bad state.
batches.outcome: A local machine-state consistency guard script, machine-readable guard artifact and static QA integration.

batches.nextMove: Run the guard after each future batch update. Keep MCQ-007 product/legal source collection as the next business workstream.

run.summary: The eighth Mission Control batch added a read-only local guard for machine-state drift and integrated it into static QA.
run.decision: Use the guard after every active Mission Control batch change. Keep repairs manual and reviewable for v0.1.

The guard checks active batch alignment across canonical queue, site feed, handoff state, priority map, prompt agent and live data.
The guard checks current batch files, generated prompt pack, browser evidence, gates and stale next-move phrases.
Loop state is a warning during active work and a strict failure after finish.
Static QA now includes the guard as a non-strict check.
The guard does not auto-repair or create external side effects.

batches.title: Product/Legal Evidence Inbox v0.1
batches.goal: Create a local evidence inbox and validator for product/legal source documents mapped to SRC-001 through SRC-009 without approving claims or changing customer-facing copy.
batches.context: Started after MCQ-009 created the Product/Legal Source Intake Pack. The source register names nine source requests, but no approved evidence records exist yet. This batch gives future work a structured place to add source documents and owner confirmations before any source status or copy can change.
batches.outcome: A local Product/Legal Evidence Inbox with incoming, records and templates folders, a machine-readable inbox artifact, a read-only validator and static QA integration.
batches.checkpoint: Review whether the inbox and validator prevent source auto-approval while making SRC-001 through SRC-009 easier to fill.
batches.nextMove: Locked/passive while Jonas's claims-work lock is active. Preserve the inbox and validator as gates, but do not add records or update source statuses until explicitly reopened.

run.summary: The tenth Mission Control batch installed a local evidence inbox and validator for product/legal source material.
run.decision: Keep the inbox as a passive gate while claims work is locked. The next active direction is MCQ-011 Run Registry + Local Eval Harness.
findings: Source collection needs an inbox before any row can move out of awaiting-source.
Evidence records require owner, date, version, excerpt, scope and storage location.
Approved source does not equal approved customer-facing copy.
The validator is read-only and integrated into static QA.
No product/legal claims were approved.
Customer-facing copy remains unchanged.

missions.purpose: Define the evidence inbox operating model.
missions.promptGoal: Turn MCQ-009 source requests into an evidence intake workflow with approval boundaries, record schema and stop rules.
missions.expectedOutput: Inbox model, record fields, source slot rules and approval boundary.
missions.validation: Compare against MCQ-009 source requests and product/legal stop rules.
missions.stopRule: Do not approve source rows, claims or copy.

missions.purpose: Build the local evidence inbox and validator.
missions.promptGoal: Create folders, templates, machine-readable inbox artifact and validate-source-intake.py.
missions.expectedOutput: Local inbox structure, JSON artifact and executable validator.
missions.validation: validate-source-intake.py passes with nine open source requests and zero approvals.
missions.stopRule: Do not store sensitive documents or mark records approved without owner evidence.

missions.purpose: Risk-check the evidence workflow.
missions.promptGoal: Review whether records can prove source scope without silently approving claims or customer copy.
missions.expectedOutput: Findings, residual risk and recommendation.
missions.validation: Check required metadata, status rules and channel boundary.
missions.stopRule: Do not convert evidence records into public claims.

missions.purpose: Fold Evidence Inbox v0.1 back into Frankly OS.
missions.promptGoal: Update Mission Control, handoff state, prompt-agent, live data, static QA, site feed, report and browser evidence.
missions.expectedOutput: Updated OS state, synced feed, prompt pack, QA results and browser screenshots.
missions.validation: Run source-intake validator, machine-state guard, static QA and browser QA.
missions.stopRule: Do not change customer-facing copy or approve product/legal claims.
